network.overall_strategy: This network follows the literature-supported systems chemistry of prebiotic decanoic acid production: hydrothermal and post-impact environments generate H2, formate, and especially CO-compatible feedstocks; Fischer-Tropsch-type synthesis on Fe/Ni-bearing metallic surfaces produces a homologous fatty-acid/amphiphile distribution that includes decanoic acid rather than deterministic free-acid chain-by-chain elongation; surface environments then concentrate and sort this mixture by evaporation and mineral adsorption; biochemical/prebiotic settings assemble mixed amphiphiles into vesicles, with decanoic acid as the key membrane-forming component. Ten distinct pathways are represented as different valid routes through shared reaction nodes, converging on decanoic acid and then on mixed amphiphile concentrate and vesicle assembly.

molecules.iupac_name: mixed amphiphile concentrate containing decanoic acid, decanol and related homologs
fatty acid mixture from carbonaceous meteorites
photochemically generated amphiphile mixture from irradiated ice analogues

reactions.name: Serpentinization-derived H2 supply

conditions.other: subsurface serpentinization; rock-water reactions in ultramafic systems generate reducing H2 flux

reactions.mechanism: Water-rock alteration during serpentinization generates H2, which becomes the reductant feeding downstream carbon reduction and FTT chemistry.
reactions.reasoning: This reaction is represented as an environmental source term rather than CO2 -> H2, correcting a previously invalid formulation. Serpentinization-generated H2 is central to Lost City-type systems and Fe/Ni-rich reducing environments (Proskurowski et al., 2008; Horita & Berndt, 1999).

reactions.name: CO2 reduction to formic acid on iron-sulfur surfaces

conditions.other: alkaline vent to subseafloor conditions; CO2 activation remains kinetically difficult but formate is observed in serpentinizing systems

reactions.mechanism: Mineral-surface reduction of CO2 by H2 yields formate/formic acid as a C1 intermediate more reactive than dissolved CO2 for later FTT-related chemistry.
reactions.reasoning: Formate is a key hydrothermal C1 intermediate and likely bridge between CO2-rich geochemistry and more reactive carbonyl feedstocks; Lost City fluids contain formate, while iron-sulfur minerals can activate CO2 even if long-chain synthesis is not demonstrated on them directly (Proskurowski et al., 2008; Santos-Carballal et al., 2018; McCollom & Seewald, 2001).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal generation of oxalic acid from reduced C1 chemistry

conditions.other: speculative connector using reduced hydrothermal C1 chemistry to link formate pools to oxalate/formate FTT feed systems

reactions.mechanism: C1 carboxyl species undergo surface-mediated coupling/oxidation-state reorganization to produce dicarboxylate feedstocks relevant to later thermal decomposition chemistry.
reactions.reasoning: Oxalic acid was used directly as an experimentally validated FTT precursor in stainless-steel reactors, but its prebiotic sourcing is less constrained. This node is therefore included as a necessary but speculative connector to integrate the McCollom precursor system into a connected network (McCollom et al., 1999).

reactions.name: Thermal decomposition of formic acid to CO feedstock

conditions.other: subsurface or post-impact hydrothermal conditions; CO-generating intermediates favored over dissolved CO2 as FTT feed

reactions.mechanism: Formic acid thermally decomposes on metallic/oxide surfaces to generate CO, providing the kinetically necessary carbonyl substrate for FTT chain growth.
reactions.reasoning: The literature emphasizes that CO or CO-generating intermediates, not dissolved CO2 alone, are the effective feedstocks for abiotic FTT lipid synthesis. Formic acid is a validated precursor in aqueous FTT experiments (McCollom et al., 1999; McCollom et al., 2010; Rushdi & Simoneit, 2001).

reactions.name: Thermal decomposition of oxalic acid to CO feedstock

conditions.other: subsurface or post-impact hydrothermal conditions; oxalic acid serves as a CO-generating precursor in steel-catalyzed lipid synthesis experiments

reactions.mechanism: Oxalic acid decomposes thermally to carbonyl fragments including CO, enriching the reactive feedstock pool for surface-mediated FTT chemistry.
reactions.reasoning: Oxalic acid was one of the specific precursors used in the landmark aqueous abiotic lipid synthesis experiments that produced homologous acids up to >C35 (McCollom et al., 1999; Rushdi & Simoneit, 2001).

reactions.name: Aqueous Fe-Ni-catalyzed Fischer-Tropsch-type synthesis of homologous amphiphiles

conditions.catalyst: Fe-Ni-Cr metallic surfaces approximated here by elemental iron (Fe0) with magnetite (Fe3O4)
conditions.other: 150-250C optimum equivalent; 2-3 days; homologous ASF-like product distribution of acids, alcohols, hydrocarbons and ketones

reactions.mechanism: Surface-bound carbonyl species undergo sequential CO insertion and hydrogenation on Fe/Ni-bearing metallic surfaces, yielding a homologous mixture of n-alkanoic acids and related amphiphiles including decanoic acid.
reactions.reasoning: This is the strongest experimentally supported route to prebiotic decanoic acid. McCollom et al. produced C2 to >C35 lipids from formic/oxalic acid in steel reactors at 175C over 2-3 days, and Rushdi & Simoneit found highest acid yields at 150-250C. The network deliberately represents homologous-distribution production rather than unsupported deterministic free-acid elongation (McCollom et al., 1999; Rushdi & Simoneit, 2001).

reactions.name: Gas-phase meteoritic-iron FTT synthesis peaking at C9-C10

conditions.catalyst: meteoritic iron analogue; K2CO3-promoted Fe surface chemistry approximated with elemental iron (Fe0)
conditions.other: CO:D2/H2 about 1:1; 24-48 h; post-impact or gas-solid reactor analogue; distribution peaks at C9-C10

reactions.mechanism: Gas-phase FTT chain growth on meteoritic/native iron forms a unimodal medium-chain fatty-acid distribution with strong production near decanoic acid.
reactions.reasoning: Nooner and Oro directly produced saturated fatty acids from C5-C18 with a peak at C9-C10 using Canyon Diablo meteoritic iron at 400C for 24-48 h. This is the most direct experimental support for decanoic acid-favored abiotic synthesis (Nooner & Oro, 1979).

reactions.name: Post-impact global FTT generation of fatty-acid mixtures on iron-rich surfaces

conditions.other: reducing post-impact atmosphere containing H2, CO, CO2; synthesis sustained over long cooling intervals

reactions.mechanism: Impact-generated reducing atmospheres and iron-rich hot surfaces operate as large-scale FTT reactors, producing total fatty-acid inventories far exceeding other sources.
reactions.reasoning: Cohen et al. quantitatively concluded that post-impact FTT synthesis on iron-rich surfaces could outproduce meteoritic delivery or spark-discharge sources by about 100-fold in total fatty-acid mass, making it a major plausible source of decanoic acid-bearing mixtures (Cohen et al., 2023).

reactions.name: Exogenous delivery of meteoritic fatty acid mixture

conditions.other: delivery to surface pools; physical influx rather than in situ synthesis; medium-chain monocarboxylic acids present at trace levels up to C12

reactions.mechanism: Meteorite infall supplies preformed fatty-acid mixtures, which are then retained and processed in surface depositional environments.
reactions.reasoning: Meteorites contain monocarboxylic acids from C2 to C12 and can deliver C10-containing mixtures, though alone they are generally insufficient to exceed vesicle-forming concentrations. This is modeled correctly as exogenous input, not chain elongation from shorter acids (Yuen & Kvenvolden, 1973; Lawless & Yuen, 1979; Huang et al., 2005; Pearce et al., 2019).

reactions.name: Exogenous UV-ice photoproduct amphiphile delivery

conditions.other: extraterrestrial photoproducts from irradiated ice analogues deposited into surface waters

reactions.mechanism: Photochemically generated amphiphiles formed in irradiated ice analogues are delivered to the planet and enter surface concentration/assembly networks.
reactions.reasoning: UV irradiation of interstellar ice analogues yields amphiphilic molecules that self-assemble into vesicles, though specific decanoic acid assignment is unresolved. This reaction is included as a literature-based exogenous amphiphile source feeding the same concentration node (Dworkin et al., 2001; Dworkin et al., 2004).

reactions.name: Surface concentration and adsorption of decanoic-acid-bearing amphiphile mixtures

conditions.other: wet-dry cycles, evaporation, mineral adsorption, and thermophoretic enrichment in hot-spring or evaporitic settings

reactions.mechanism: Decanoic acid and co-produced amphiphiles are concentrated from dilute synthesis mixtures by adsorption to clays, evaporation in pools, and related physical sorting processes, producing a membrane-competent mixed amphiphile reservoir.
reactions.reasoning: The concentration gap is a central bottleneck. Montmorillonite strongly adsorbs fatty acids and promotes assembly, while evaporation and gradient-driven accumulation are leading proposed concentration mechanisms. The reaction represents physical concentration rather than unsupported covalent chemistry (Hanczyc et al., 2003; Budin et al., 2009; Damer & Deamer, 2020; Pearce et al., 2019).

reactions.name: Biochemical/prebiotic mixed amphiphile vesicle assembly

conditions.other: freshwater to mildly saline conditions; pH about 6.5-9 for decanoic-acid-rich systems; mixed amphiphiles lower CVC and improve thermal robustness

reactions.mechanism: Self-assembly of concentrated decanoic-acid-rich mixtures into vesicles occurs once the effective critical vesicle concentration is crossed; heterogeneous amphiphiles strongly lower this threshold and broaden pH/salt tolerance.
reactions.reasoning: Decanoic acid is the practical minimum straight-chain fatty acid for robust protocell membranes, but mixed amphiphile systems greatly improve assembly. Jordan et al. showed C10-C15 mixtures form vesicles under vent-relevant conditions, while Budin et al. showed heterogeneity dramatically lowers CVC (Jordan et al., 2019; Budin et al., 2014; Groen et al., 2012; Namani & Walde, 2005; Mansy & Szostak, 2008).

pathways.name: Hydrothermal formate-to-aqueous-FTT route
pathways.description: CO2 is reduced to formic acid in hydrothermal systems, then converted to CO, which undergoes aqueous Fe/Ni-type FTT synthesis to yield decanoic acid within a homologous product distribution.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal oxalate-bridged aqueous FTT route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal reduced C1 chemistry generates oxalic acid, which decomposes to CO and feeds aqueous FTT synthesis producing decanoic acid among longer homologs.

pathways.name: Formate-to-meteoritic-iron gas-phase FTT route
pathways.description: Hydrothermally generated formate is converted to CO and then processed in a higher-temperature gas-phase meteoritic/native iron FTT regime that peaks near C9-C10 fatty acids.

pathways.name: Oxalate-to-meteoritic-iron gas-phase FTT route
pathways.description: Oxalic acid serves as a CO-generating precursor for high-temperature gas-phase FTT chemistry on iron-rich catalytic surfaces, yielding decanoic acid-rich medium-chain products.

pathways.name: Formate-to-post-impact global FTT route
pathways.description: Formate-derived CO enters a post-impact large-scale FTT regime on iron-rich hot surfaces, producing decanoic acid as part of a broad amphiphile distribution.

pathways.name: Oxalate-to-post-impact global FTT route
pathways.description: Oxalate-derived CO participates in iron-rich post-impact FTT chemistry that supplies decanoic acid and related amphiphiles at higher total flux than other known sources.

pathways.name: Aqueous FTT to surface concentration to protocells
pathways.description: Decanoic acid formed in aqueous hydrothermal FTT chemistry is transported to surface pools, concentrated with co-amphiphiles, and assembled into vesicles.

pathways.name: Gas-phase C9-C10-peaked FTT to surface concentration to protocells
pathways.description: A meteoritic/native-iron gas-phase FTT regime directly favors medium-chain products near decanoic acid, after which surface concentration yields vesicle-forming mixtures.

pathways.name: Post-impact FTT plus meteoritic augmentation route
pathways.description: Post-impact FTT-generated decanoic acid is augmented in surface pools by delivered meteoritic fatty acids, then concentrated on minerals and assembled into protocells.

pathways.name: Post-impact FTT plus UV-ice amphiphile augmentation route
pathways.description: Post-impact or hydrothermal decanoic acid production combines with extraterrestrial UV-generated amphiphiles in surface pools, where concentration enables mixed-membrane assembly.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Strong literature-aligned core route: serpentinization supplies H2, formate can serve as CO-generating precursor, and aqueous Fe-Ni-catalyzed FTT chemistry is the best-supported route to decanoic acid as part of a homologous distribution. Main weakness is that the path ends at decanoic acid production without explicitly addressing concentration to vesicle-relevant levels.
suggestions: Specify the exact catalyst composition for rxn_006 (e.g., Fe-Ni-Cr steel, awaruite, native Fe/Ni) and temperature window closer to 150-250C literature optimum
Add explicit concentration/transport steps if this pathway is intended to support membrane formation rather than just synthesis

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This route is plausible because oxalic acid has experimental relevance as a CO-generating precursor for FTT-like synthesis, but the specific conversion of formate to oxalate under hydrothermal prebiotic conditions is much less directly supported than the rest of the pathway. The downstream CO-to-decanoic-acid logic is sound.
suggestions: Justify rxn_003 with stronger literature support or mark it explicitly as speculative
If possible, derive oxalate from a better-supported geochemical source rather than from formate alone

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Among the strongest routes. The Nooner and Oro-type meteoritic iron gas-phase FTT system is directly relevant because it reportedly peaks around C9-C10, matching the target unusually well. The route is chemically and literature-wise robust, though still dependent on access to CO rather than CO2 directly.
suggestions: Clarify whether this is truly hydrothermal or better classified as impact-associated/high-temperature surface or post-impact chemistry
Include K2CO3 promoter or explain omission if following Nooner and Oro closely

pathway_evaluations.feedback: A reasonably coherent variant of P3, but again the weakest point is the formate-to-oxalate step. Once CO is available, the meteoritic-iron FTT route to decanoic acid is one of the most literature-consistent parts of the network.
suggestions: Strengthen or replace rxn_003
Better separate pre-CO generation chemistry from the experimentally supported FTT stage

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This is very well aligned with the deep research findings, especially the modern quantitative emphasis on post-impact FTT as the highest-yield source of prebiotic fatty acids. It captures the systems-chemistry idea that decanoic acid emerges from a mixture rather than selective single-product synthesis.
suggestions: Explicitly note post-impact reducing atmosphere and hot iron-rich surfaces in the reaction details
Quantify that decanoic acid is only a fraction of the total product mixture and requires concentration

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Good overall logic and good agreement with post-impact FTT literature, but again weakened by uncertain oxalate generation from formate. The rest of the route is among the most plausible in the network.
suggestions: Treat oxalate as an externally available precursor if literature support is stronger that way
Provide stronger geochemical reasoning for oxalate accumulation before FTT

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This is one of the best full-environment pathways because it carries synthesis through concentration into vesicle assembly, matching the literature's multi-environment sequential scenario. The main limitation is that rxn_011 compresses several difficult processes—sorting, adsorption, evaporation, and achieving vesicle-relevant concentrations—into a single underspecified step.
suggestions: Break rxn_011 into distinct transport, evaporation, and mineral adsorption steps
Include a mineral such as montmorillonite explicitly and note low-ionic-strength conditions before vesicle assembly

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This is the strongest end-to-end pathway in the network. It combines the most target-focused synthesis step (C9-C10-peaked meteoritic-iron FTT) with plausible surface concentration and mixed amphiphile vesicle assembly. It still abstracts the concentration problem, but overall it tracks the literature very closely.
suggestions: Make the concentration mechanism explicit: evaporation, thermophoresis, or montmorillonite adsorption
Include mixed amphiphile composition constraints for vesicle formation, such as decanoic acid/decanol/glycerol monodecanoate ratios

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Excellent systems-level pathway. It reflects the literature consensus that post-impact FTT supplies the bulk of fatty acids while meteoritic input supplements diversity and possibly membrane performance. This pathway also directly addresses the concentration gap better than synthesis-only routes.
suggestions: Explicitly note that meteoritic delivery alone is insufficient but helpful as an augmenting source
Clarify whether exogenous input is enhancing concentration, compositional diversity, or both

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This is a good systems pathway, but weaker than P9 because UV-ice photoproduct mixtures are literature-supported as amphiphile sources without specific confirmation of decanoic acid itself. Still, as a mixed-amphiphile augmentation route to vesicle assembly, it is plausible.
suggestions: Frame UV-ice products as amphiphile analog supplementation rather than direct decanoic acid delivery
Specify how these photoproducts improve vesicle assembly or lower CVC in the mixed system

evaluation.network_feedback:
The network is conceptually strong and broadly aligned with current prebiotic decanoic acid literature. Its main strength is that it treats decanoic acid as a member of a heterogeneous FTT-derived amphiphile distribution rather than a uniquely selected end product, which matches the evidence very well. Topologically, the network is connected and has clear convergence around formate, CO, decanoic acid, and mixed amphiphile concentrate. The hydrothermal-to-surface-to-biochemical flow is also present and consistent with multi-environment scenarios emphasized in the literature.

However, several factors keep the overall confidence below approval threshold. First, the network description omits required detailed JSON-level reaction metadata, especially explicit mineral agents for all hydrothermal and surface reactions. From an evaluation standpoint, this is a structural compliance issue and also weakens environmental specificity. Second, rxn_003 (formate to oxalate) is the least supported chemical step and is used in multiple pathways. Third, rxn_011 overcompresses the major unresolved concentration problem identified in the literature: reaching membrane-forming concentrations of C10-bearing amphiphiles is not a trivial single step. Fourth, the classification of some high-temperature gas-phase/post-impact chemistry under 'Hydrothermal' is somewhat blurred. Finally, the network lacks per-reaction literature citations in the reasoning fields, which is a key requirement when deep research findings are provided.

Overall, this is a good and scientifically informed network, especially for systems-level thinking, but it falls short of excellent because the weakest steps are exactly where the literature says the real prebiotic bottlenecks lie: CO generation, selective medium-chain yield, and concentration to vesicle-forming levels.
